segments.s608-g17: وزان سوی آتش is a compact spatial linking phrase: وزان (poetic and/from that) + سوی (here 'side/direction of') + آتش ('fire'). Together they mean 'on/at that side of the fire' and function as a scene-setting adverbial.
segments.s608-g18: This clause states the action: یکی (a single/one) + جشنگه (festival‑place) is qualified by ساخته ('made/laid out'); بر کران ('on the edge/bank') is the locative phrase. Subject (همه دختران from previous segment) is understood; ساخته is the past participle/past verb meaning 'they made/laid out'.
segments.s608-g19: ز گل is a poetic 'of/from flowers' construction indicating material or ornament; هر یکی ('each one') is a distributive phrase placing emphasis on individuality; بر سرش افسری is the attributive phrase 'a diadem upon her head' with ش as the possessive suffix 'her'.
segments.s608-g20: نشسته is a predicative past participle meaning 'sitting/settled'; به هرجای is a locative/adverbial ('to/in whatever place' or 'wherever'); رامشگری ('tranquility/peaceful repose') names the kind of place—together: 'they sat wherever they rested in peace.'
segments.s608-g21: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/kept on'; چامه رزم خسرو is a nominal phrase 'the poem/lay of Khosrow's battle' (چامه modified by رزم خسرو); زدند is the past plural verb 'they struck/sounded/performed'—here used for sounding a song or tune.
segments.s608-g22: وزان ('and/from that') links to the previous scene; جایگه ('place') is the source/location; هر زمان ('every time') + نو ('new') modifies زدند (again 'they struck/sang'), so the clause means 'from that place they at every moment struck/sang something new.'
segments.s608-g23: This is a paired nominal description using repeated همه for emphasis: ماه‌روی is a compound adjective 'moon‑faced' (i.e., beautiful‑faced) and جعدموی 'ringleted/with curls'; the clause is nominal and elliptical (copula 'were' is omitted but implied).
segments.s608-g24: Another paired nominal phrase: جامه گوهر is 'robe/garment of jewels' (noun+noun where the second specifies material/ornament) and مشک موی is 'musk‑hair'—an idiomatic way to say their hair smelled of musk; again the copula is omitted and understood.
segments.s608-g81: چنین (thus) is an adverb placing how the reply was given. داد is the past tense of ‘to give’ and with پاسخ (answer) forms the verbal phrase ‘gave an answer’. ورا (to him) is an enclitic/dative pronoun placed after the verb phrase, and پیرمرد (the old man) names the speaker; word order is literary/poetic (adverb — verb — object — dative — subject).
segments.s608-g82: کزین در is a fused classical locution meaning ‘from this gate/that door’ (کزین = از + این). که introduces the relative clause ‘which you said’ (گفتی would normally be ‘you said’ here), and سوارا is the plural/collective object ‘the riders/horsemen’. مگرد is a negative imperative/prohibitive (‘do not turn/drive away’); the clause as a whole gives the command not to turn the riders away from the door mentioned.
segments.s608-g83: نه introduces a series of negations (‘neither... nor’). جا هست ما را is an inverted existential construction meaning ‘there is no place for us’ or colloquially ‘we have no place’ (هست = exist/be; ما را = to us / for us). بوم و بر is a fixed pairing in Persian meaning ‘homeland and land’ (here both negated), and the coordinating و links them.
segments.s608-g84: The speaker continues the list of things they lack: نه سیم و سرای (neither silver nor dwelling/house) and نه گاو و نه خر (neither cow nor donkey). و is the coordinating ‘and’. Note that سرای here means a house/abode (classical usage), not the modern poetic lemma listed in the metadata.
segments.s608-g85: بدو (to him) marks the indirect object ‘to him’. گفت is the past tense ‘said’ and introduces Bahram’s speech; بهرام is the speaker’s name. شاید is an adverb ‘perhaps’. مرا here sets up the person affected by the following clause (‘me’ / ‘they will need me’), placed before the subordinate clause for emphasis.
segments.s608-g86: که introduces the content of Bahram’s thought (‘that’). بی‌چیز ایشان = ‘their being without possessions’ or ‘they are poor’ (بی‌چیز = needy). بباید is a modal/impersonal form meaning ‘it will be necessary’ / ‘they will have need’ (here: ‘they will need’). مرا را (split in the word list as م + را) is the object ‘me’ (مرا) and the enclitic را is the object/definiteness marker; together they indicate ‘they will need me’.
segments.s608-g87: بدو گفت (to him said) again introduces speech. هرچار is a compound meaning ‘each of the four’ / ‘all four’, and جفت means ‘pair’ or ‘couple’; together they mean ‘each of the four pairs’ or ‘the four pairs’. تواند is the present/subjunctive/ability form ‘can’ / ‘is able to’, expressing capability.
segments.s608-g88: پرستارگان is the plural of پرستار (attendants, caregivers) — here the objects that can be kept/hidden. نهفت is an adjectival/participle form meaning ‘hidden, concealed’ (it modifies or complements the object), and تواند repeats the modal ‘can’ (literary repetition/emphasis): together, ‘can hide/keep attendants concealed.’
segments.s608-g137: بفرمای is an archaic/impertive/formal request (literally 'do please/issue') addressed to a ruler; it precedes the noun فرمان ('order') as the verb. که introduces the following clause ('that/for'), here linking the command to its reason or content. پیمان تراست is a verbless-like stative clause: پیمان 'a pledge/covenant' + تراست ('is yours'), with تراست functioning as a possessive/stative predicate meaning 'belongs to you' or 'is yours'.
segments.s608-g138: همه بندگانیم is a simple predication 'we are all servants' (همه 'all/everyone' + بندگانیم 'we are servants' where -یم marks 1st person plural). و simply connects the clauses, and فرمان تراست repeats the idea 'the command/authority is yours' with فرمان 'command' as subject and تراست 'is yours' as the stative predicate.
segments.s608-g139: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. ما 'we' is the subject; همه 'all' qualifies it. کهتران is the plural/comparative 'the lesser/inferior ones' (classical formation), used here as a noun meaning 'inferiors' — its provided sense list doesn't match, so the correct sense is not in the list. توایم is an archaic 1st-person form meaning 'I am yours' but used with ما to mean 'we are yours' (the verb form is historically singular but used collectively).
segments.s608-g140: چه here is an exclamatory/causal particle ('for/indeed' or 'for even'), introducing the explanatory clause. کهتر 'the lesser/lowliest' (adjective/noun) repeats the idea; همه 'all' intensifies; چاکران is plural 'attendants/servants' and توایم again expresses 'we (am) yours' — idiomatically: 'for even the humblest of us are your attendants.'
segments.s608-g141: بدو is a short prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'to him' (not the imperative 'run!') placed before the subject for emphasis. آسیابان 'the miller(s)' and زن 'the woman' form the coordinated subject; خیره ماند is a past/resultative phrase 'remained astonished/stood amazed' (ماند 'remained/stayed'). Word order (to him — miller and woman — remained astonished) is typical of older Persian.
segments.s608-g142: همی is a narrative/inchoative particle often meaning 'then/indeed/kept on' and here marks the action that follows. هر یکی 'each one' is a distributive subject; نام یزدان 'the name of God' is the object. بخواند (3rd‑person imperfect/habitual in classical style) means 'would call/cry out (the name of God)' — here translated as 'called upon the name of God.'
segments.s608-g143: چنین 'thus' introduces direct speech; گفت here is the speech verb 'said' (the provided senses list did not include the verbal sense, so that entry is not matched). مهتر 'the elder/chief' is the speaker. که introduces the reported content ('that'). آن ('that/the') modifies روی و موی 'face and hair' as the topic of the reported clause.
segments.s608-g144: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; چرخ here means 'heaven/sky' (the celestial sphere). خور is 'the sun' (agent). آورد 'brought' is the perfective verb 'brought/carry'; شوی is a classical auxiliary/passive marker from شدن ('to become / to be') used here to form a passive/result ('were brought' / 'became brought'), so the whole phrase reads 'were brought by the sun from the fourth heaven.'
segments.s608-g41: This clause has an implicit subject (the king) and a direct-object structure finishing with the verb دید (past 'saw'). همه دشت = 'the whole plain' (همه modifies دشت). یکسر is an adverb intensifying پر (full), and پر از + noun = 'full of ...' (پر از ماه = 'full of beauties' — ماه here is poetic for 'beautiful one').
segments.s608-g42: به شهر آمدن is a verbal-noun phrase 'the coming to the city' (به + infinitive/masdar), followed by راه کوتاه (noun + adjective) and the verb دید 'he saw/found'. Word order is [masdar phrase] [noun phrase] [verb], meaning 'he saw that the way to the city's coming was short' → 'he saw that the road to the city was short.'
segments.s608-g43: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces the subordinate clause (so that/that), followed by میگساران 'the cupbearers' and ز راه 'from the road' (ز = poetic 'from'). The clause marks an order for approach/entry.
segments.s608-g44: می‌آرند is used as the verb of the subordinate clause 'they come/are to come' (present/habitual or subjunctive-realized sense after تا). The conjunction و links a second request: میخواره 'the wine‑drinker/drunkard' is the noun; نزدیک شاه 'near the king' gives the destination — the verb for the second conjunct is understood (elliptical) as 'bring/bring near'.
segments.s608-g45: گسارنده is an agent noun (the pourer/cupbearer) preceding the past verb آورد 'brought' (subject–verb order). جام بلور is a noun + noun/adjective phrase where بلور (crystal) qualifies جام (cup): 'a crystal cup.'
segments.s608-g46: نهادند is the past plural verb 'they placed/put' (third-person plural). بر دست = 'on/into the hand' (بر = on/upon/toward), and بهرام گور is the recipient name (Bahrām Gūr); گور here is an epithet (onager) attached to the name.
segments.s608-g47: ازان دختران = 'of those maidens' (ازان = 'of those/from those'). آنک is a relative 'who/which' referring to those maidens; بد نامدار is a compound attributive phrase ('badly-named' / 'ill‑reputed' / 'notorious') modifying the relative. The phrase identifies which of the maidens are meant.
segments.s608-g48: برون آمدند is the past plural verb 'came forth' (برون = out/forth + آمدند = came), here agreeing with a plural subject implied by آنک. از میانه = 'from the midst' and میانه چهار = 'of the four' (i.e. out of the four maidens). Word order is object-of-origin + verb.
segments.s608-g97: سپاه اندر آمد یکایک ز دشت = A compact classical word order: سپاه (the army/host) is the subject, اندر آمد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'came in/entered' (اندر = in/into + آمد = came), and یکایک ('one by one' / 'each one') modifies the manner of arrival; ز دشت ('from the plain') is a prepositional source phrase placed after it. The sequence shows typical Persian flexibility (manner and source phrases can follow the verb).
segments.s608-g98: همه شب همی دشت لشکر گذشت = 'همه شب' ('the whole night') is a temporal adverbial; همی is the old continuative particle marking an ongoing or repeated past action ('kept / was continually'); دشت لشکر گذشت literally 'the host passed the plain' with گذشت as the simple past 'passed' and دشت ('the plain') functioning as the place crossed. Word order places the time first and the verb at the end, a common classical pattern.
segments.s608-g99: فروماند زان آسیابان شگفت = فروماند is a past-state verb meaning 'was worn out/left exhausted' (passive/state result); زان = 'from that' (referring back to something previously mentioned); آسیابان here means 'the miller(s)' and شگفت is an adjective 'marvelous/strange' describing the miller. The phrase as a whole says he was exhausted because of that astonishing miller.
segments.s608-g100: شب تیره اندیشه اندر گرفت = 'شب تیره' is a compound nominal phrase 'the dark/ominous night'; اندیشه ('thought') is the noun that follows; اندر گرفت is a light-verb construction meaning 'took hold of' or 'seized' (اندر = in/on, گرفت = took/seized). Together it reads idiomatically as 'a dark night of thought took hold (of him)'.
segments.s608-g101: به زن گفت کاین نامدار چو ماه = به زن گفت is a simple past clause 'he said to the woman' (به = to, زن = woman, گفت = said). کاین is the classical complementizer 'that/this' introducing reported speech; نامدار is the predicate noun 'renowned/famed', and چو ماه is a simile 'like the moon' modifying نامدار. The clause is elliptic in Persian: the copula 'is' is implied.
segments.s608-g102: بدین برز بالا و این دستگاه = بدین here functions as an archaism meaning 'by/with/at this' (classical fused prepositional usage), برز بالا is an attribute meaning 'a high/exalted rank or station' (برز = height/exaltation, بالا = high/lofty), and و این دستگاه adds 'and this retinue/establishment' (دستگاه = court/entourage). The phrase completes the reported description of the famed man's status and household.
segments.s608-g103: شب تیره بر آسیا چون رسید = شب تیره ('the dark night') is the temporal subject; بر آسیا ('on/over the mill') is a locative phrase indicating where the night fell; چون رسید is a classical temporal clause 'when it came/reached' (چون = when/as, رسید = arrived/reached). Together: 'When the dark night came upon the mill...'.
segments.s608-g104: زنش گفت کز دور آتش بدید = زنش is زن + possessive suffix ش 'his wife' (or 'his woman'); گفت introduces reported speech 'said'; کز (که + ز) is a classical complementizer 'that/from which' i.e. 'that'; دور آتش 'a fire at a distance' (دور = far, آتش = fire); بدید is the past form 'saw'. So: 'His wife said that (she) saw a fire far off.'
segments.s608-g121: چو ... شد is a subordinating clause meaning "when ... became" (چو = when). شب روز شد = "night became day." مهتر آمد به ده is the main clause: مهتر (the chief/headman) is the subject and آمد is the past-tense verb "came," followed by the prepositional phrase به ده "to the village/into the village." The sequence shows a poetic/inverted ordering but follows normal past narration.
segments.s608-g122: بدین پیر گفتا = an archaic phrasing of به این پیر گفت ("he said to this/that elder"); ب + دین here functions as the older contracted form of به این. گفتا is the simple past "said." که introduces the reported speech (complementizer), and ای is the vocative particle "O" addressing روزبه (Rouzbeh). In short: "he said to that elder: 'O Rouzbeh...'"
segments.s608-g123: به بالینت آمد شب تیره‌بخت places the locative phrase first: به بالینت "to/at your pillow/bedside." آمد is the past-tense verb "came," and شب تیره‌بخت "the ill‑fated night" is the subject noun phrase (شب modified by the compound adjective تیره‑بخت). The ordering is stylistic inversion common in poetry.
segments.s608-g124: به بار آمد is a verbal phrase meaning "came to bear / came laden / bore fruit" (به بار + آمد), i.e. "came into bearing." آن سبز شاخ درخت is the noun phrase subject: آن (that) + سبز (green) + شاخ (branch, bough) + درخت (of the tree). The genitive relation is shakh-e derakht (branch of the tree), though the connective -e is omitted in poetic compression.
segments.s608-g125: شب تیره‌گون دوش = a compact, poetic time/location phrase: شب تیره‌گون (a dark- or gloomy-hued night) + دوش (here meaning "last night"). بهرامشاه follows as the proper-name subject. In classical Persian دوش commonly means "yesterday/last night."
segments.s608-g126: همی آمد is an older/perfective-progressive form where همی gives iterative or continuous flavor ("was coming" / "indeed came"); آمد is the past verb "came." از دشت نخچیرگاه is the source phrase "from the plain of the hunting-ground" (از = from; دشت = plain; نخچیرگاه = hunting-ground).
segments.s608-g127: نگه کرد is a periphrastic expression (نگه "gaze/looking" + کرد "did") meaning "he looked" or "he fixed his gaze." این جشن و آتش بدید = "he saw this feast and the fire" (این = this; جشن = celebration; آتش = fire; بدید = saw). The two verbs are coordinated narration: he gazed and (then) saw.
segments.s608-g128: عنان را بپیچید = "he turned the reins" (عنان = reins, را marks the direct object, بپیچید is the past of پیچیدن used here as 'to turn'/'to twist'). و زین سو کشید = "and he pulled/drew off toward that side" (زین = saddle; سو = side/toward; کشید is past of کشیدن 'to pull/draw' — here meaning he pulled the reins/rode off in that direction). The object marker را makes عنان explicit as the thing acted on.
segments.s608-g89: The phrase begins with the preposition به marking the domain or respect ('with regard to / in'), followed by the coordinated nouns عیب و هنر ('fault and virtue'). چشم تو is the possessor + noun ('your eye' / 'your perception') and دیدشان is the past tense verb دید with the object clitic -شان ('saw them' / 'perceived them'). Word order is prepositional phrase → possessor+noun → verb+enclitic, a common archaic/poetic sequence.
segments.s608-g90: بدین‌سان که introduces a manner/result clause ('in this way, such that'); که introduces the subordinate clause. دیدی is second-person past 'you saw'. پسندیده‌شان is a past-participle form used predicatively with the object clitic -شان ('(you) found/approved them'); in older Persian a participle can stand as the main predicate, so the sequence means 'you saw them thus and found them pleasing'.
segments.s608-g91: بدو گفت بهرام is a fronting of the indirect-object/dative (بدو 'to him') before the reporting verb گفت and the speaker بهرام: 'to him said Bahram' = 'Bahram said to him'. کاین هر چهار = که این هر چهار ('that these four/every one of the four') introduces the content of the speech.
segments.s608-g92: پذیرفتم is first-person past 'I accepted/received/acknowledged'. از marks source/agent 'from', and پاک پروردگار is a noun phrase 'the Pure Lord' in which پاک ('pure') modifies پروردگار ('Lord'). The whole clause expresses Bahram's statement of reception/acceptance from God.
segments.s608-g93: بگفت این = 'he said this' (بگفت as narrative past 'he said/declared', این = 'this/that [just said]'). و از جای بر پای خاست is an idiomatic motion clause: از جای 'from his place' + بر پای خاست 'rose to his feet/stand up', where خاست is past 'arose' and بر پای is literal 'onto (his) feet'.
segments.s608-g94: به دشت اندر = 'into/onto the plain' (به ... اندر = movement/locative phrase). آوای بالای خاست is an intransitive clause 'a high/strong voice arose' (آوا = voice/cry, بالای = 'high/strong' in poetic use, خاست = 'arose'). The verb is intransitive past, so the phrase describes a loud cry issuing over the plain.
segments.s608-g95: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past used to report a command or utterance ('he commanded/said'); تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/command ('that' / 'so that'). خادمان سپاه is a genitive or descriptive compound 'the attendants/servants of the army'.
segments.s608-g96: برند is the third-person plural (subjunctive/imperative flavor) following تا ('that they carry' / 'let them carry') from بردن 'to carry'. آن بتان را is the definite object ('those idols' + object marker را). به مشکوی شاه is the destination phrase 'to the musk-scented king', with مشکوی an adjective 'musk-scented' modifying شاه.
segments.s608-g25: This is an adverbial location phrase. به نزدیک (literally “to/very near”) intensifies vicinity; پیش در آسیا literally ‘in front of the door of the mill’ — here در is the noun “door” not the preposition, and آسیا is “mill.” The whole phrase means “near, before the mill’s door.”
segments.s608-g26: This is the remainder of the adverbial clause describing what was there. به رامش gives purpose or result (“for repose/for calm”), کشیده is a past participle of کشیدن meaning “stretched/drawn,” and نخی is the noun “a thin cord/thread” being modified by کشیده. بر گیا is a locative phrase “upon/over the grass/vegetation.” Word order is participle after the purpose phrase and before its object noun.
segments.s608-g27: وزان is a connective “and/from that (because of that),” linking to the preceding scene. هر یکی = “each one.” دسته گل به دست is a noun phrase meaning “a bundle/bouquet of flowers in (her) hand”; به here functions idiomatically with دست to indicate location (‘in the hand’).
segments.s608-g28: ز شادی و از می‌شده gives the causes of their condition: ز شادی “from/with joy” and از می‌شده literally ‘having been from wine’ — here می‌شده is the compound (می + شده) built with شدن and means “made/caused by wine” (classical phrasing). نیم‌مست is the predicative adjective “half‑drunk/half‑intoxicated,” describing those (هر یکی) mentioned before.
segments.s608-g29: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial “after that.” خروش آمد is a subject–verb clause (خروش “a cry/uproar” before the verb آمد “came/rose”), and از جشنگاه marks the source/location “from the festival/celebratory place.” The word order (noun + آمد) is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s608-g30: که introduces the content of the cry (reported clause). جاوید is an adjective “eternal.” ماناد is an archaic/poetic past-form of ماندن used optatively/hortatively here — effectively “may he remain” or “let him remain.” بهرامشاه is the name being invoked. Together they reproduce the shout: “May Bahramshah remain eternal.”
segments.s608-g31: که begins a relative clause describing Bahramshah (“who…”). با فر و برزست pairs the instrumental phrase با فر (“with farr,” i.e. royal/divine glory) with the verb برزست (archaic past of بر‌خاست/برزیدن) meaning “rose/exalted.” و با مهر و چهر adds another coordinated instrumental phrase “and with favour/kindness and with face/beauty,” continuing attributes by which he is exalted.
segments.s608-g32: برویست is an archaic/polite verb form meaning “rests/is placed upon” or “appears upon” (third person). بر پای گردان سپهر is a poetic locative phrase “on the foot/base of the revolving sky” (گردان سپهر = the turning celestial sphere). The verb precedes the prepositional phrase in classical poetic word order.
segments.s608-g33: همی is an adverb of continuity placed before the verb to mean ‘continually/always.’ می‌چکد is 3rd-person singular present/habitual ‘it drips’ (here impersonal, referring to something like perfume or moisture). گویی is a parenthetical simile ‘as if’ (poetic/archaic) introducing the source phrase. از + روی introduces the source ‘from the face,’ and اوی is an archaic enclitic possessive ‘her’ modifying روی.
segments.s608-g34: همی again marks continuous action. بوی مشک is the noun phrase ‘the scent of musk’ serving as subject. آید is 3rd-person present ‘comes/rises’ describing the emanation. از موی اوی marks the source ‘from her hair,’ with موی = ‘hair’ and اوی the possessive ‘her.’ Word order is standard Persian: subject + verb, with the source introduced by از.
segments.s608-g35: شکارش is شکار (quarry/prey) with the enclitic -ش ‘his’ — literally ‘his quarry.’ نباشد here is classical negative copula meaning ‘there is not’ or ‘he has no…’ (classical present). جز از introduces the exception ‘except from’ and is followed by two nouns joined by و. شیر = ‘lion’; گور in this epic context means the wild ass/onager (not ‘grave’).
segments.s608-g36: خوانند is 3rd-person plural present/habitual ‘they call’ (classical narrative present used to state a customary name). ازیراش is an archaic/adverbial phrase functioning causally or referentially (‘because of this’ / ‘for that reason’ — the form is obscure/poetic). بهرام گور is the name assigned: بهرام (proper name) + گور (here ‘Gūr’ = onager) forming the epithet.
segments.s608-g37: جهاندار is the subject ‘the ruler/sovereign.’ کاواز ایشان is the object phrase ‘their cry/call’ (کاواز here likely a call or signal) with ایشان ‘their.’ شنید is simple past ‘heard.’ Word order is subject + object + verb, a common classical Persian order.
segments.s608-g38: عنان را uses را as the definite object marker attached to عنان ‘the reins.’ بپیچید is past tense (he twisted/turned) with the prefix بـ- marking the verb; و links the two coordinated actions. زان سو is a locative/directional phrase ‘that way/in that direction’ (زان = ‘that’/‘from that’ + سو = ‘side/toward’). کشید is past of کشیدن ‘to pull’ — here ‘he pulled (the reins).’
segments.s608-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when.’ آمد is simple past ‘he came.’ به نزدیکی دختران is a prepositional/time/place phrase ‘to the proximity of the maidens’ (به + نزدیکی = ‘to the vicinity of’). The construction places the temporal clause before the main action, as common in epic narration.
segments.s608-g40: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (a noun ‘gaze/watch’) + کرد (past ‘did/made’) = ‘he gazed/looked.’ جای here means ‘the place/scene’ (what he looked at). از کران تا کران is an idiomatic interval ‘from edge to edge,’ with از...تا... marking the span; تا functions as ‘as far as’/‘to,’ so the whole phrase means he surveyed the place all around.
segments.s608-g9: برزگان لشکر is a construct phrase 'the chiefs/notables of the army' (برزگان as possessive/qualifier + لشکر). همی is the archaic progressive/aspect particle (indicating continuous or habitual action) placed before the past plural verb راندند (3rd person plural past) meaning 'drove' or 'led'. Word order is literary S‑O‑V/compound nominal + verb.
segments.s608-g10: سخنهای شاهنشهان is a genitive/attributive sequence 'the words/speeches of the shahanshahs (kings of kings)'; the verb خواندند is past 3rd plural 'they read/recited'. In classical epic style the object phrase precedes the verb and خواندند denotes recitation/read‑aloud here.
segments.s608-g11: یکی is the indefinite subject 'a certain one/someone'; آتشی is the noun 'a fire' with the adjectival -i, رخشان is a postpositive adjective 'radiant/bright'. ز دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar.' دید is simple past 'saw/was seen' (here narrative past). The order is literary: subject + object(+adj) + verb + adjunct.
segments.s608-g12: بران سان که forms a result/comparative clause 'in/so on that manner that…' (بران سان = 'thereon/so'); که introduces the ensuing clause. بهمن is used as a proper name (Bahman) and کند is the classical narrative present/past verb 'does/makes' (here 'made' or 'held'). شاه سور is 'kingly feast' or 'the king's banquet' (سور = feast). Overall this is a result clause: 'so [bright] that Bahman the king held a feast.'
segments.s608-g13: شهنشاه is the subject 'the emperor/sovereign.' بر روشنی is a prepositional phrase 'toward/at the light' (بر + noun). بنگرید is a literary past/formal-gaze form of the verb 'to look/gaze' (3rd person singular). The clause reads 'the emperor looked toward the light.'
segments.s608-g14: به یک‌سو is a directional phrase 'to one side' or 'to one direction.' دهی here functions in the text as the noun 'ده' (a village) that appears; the provided lemma senses do not list 'village', so none of the supplied senses match (see note). خرم is an adjective 'joyful/pleasant' modifying دهی, آمد is past 'came' and پدید is a literary perfective 'appeared/manifested' — together 'a joyful village came into view/to one side appeared.'
segments.s608-g15: یکی again is the indefinite 'a certain one/someone.' آسیا is the noun 'mill.' دید is past 'saw/was seen.' در پیش is a fixed locative phrase 'in front of / before' and ده here means 'village' (compound: 'in front of the village'). Word order: subject + object + verb + locative phrase (poetic variation).
segments.s608-g16: نشسته and پراگنده are participial/adjectival forms 'seated' and 'scattered' describing مردان (men). مه is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning 'great' or 'mickle' placed after the noun in apposition: 'the great men.' The sequence gives a picturesque word order: 'seated, scattered, the great men.'
segments.s608-g113: به روی زمین is a prepositional phrase meaning 'onto/upon the surface of the earth' (به + روی + زمین). بر here reinforces position 'on/upon', همی is an archaic/adverbial particle adding immediacy or emphasis, ماه is the grammatical subject, and جست is the short past form 'leapt/appeared' — a poetic intransitive verb placed after its subject.
segments.s608-g114: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...' with the two objects (دینار and دختر شاه) placed before the verb, a common poetic word order. جست here is used as a transitive past 'sought' with an elided subject carried over from the preceding narrative.
segments.s608-g115: بت آرا is a compound epithet (بت + آرا) meaning 'the adorned idol / the adorned beauty'. ببینـد (ببیند) is the 3rd singular narrative/present form often used in epic storytelling and here functions as 'he saw' or 'when he saw'; چو = 'when', ایشان = 'they/those', and به چین places them 'into wrinkles' or 'with folds' (چین = wrinkle/fold).
segments.s608-g116: گسسته is a predicative adjective 'broken/undone' and شود is the subjunctive/passive auxiliary 'becomes/is made'. بر + بتان indicates the target ('upon the beauties/idols'), and آفرین here functions as 'praise/acclamation' (a noun). Grammatically this is a predicative clause: '(the) praise upon the beauties became broken/ceased.'
segments.s608-g117: برین گونه is an archaic phrase = بر این گونه 'in this manner'. تا introduces the result/purpose 'so that' or 'until', شید is the affected person (the 'beloved' or love‑mad one) as subject, and بر پشت راغ is a poetic phrase 'upon the back of longing' (راغ = desire/longing). The verb of motion that completes the thought follows in the next segment.
segments.s608-g118: برآمد is an intransitive past 'arose/appeared'. جهان is the subject 'the world', and شد is the copula 'became'. چو = 'as/like' introduces a simile; روشن چراغ is an inverted adjective + noun 'a bright lamp' (adjective before noun, common in poetry).
segments.s608-g119: همی رفت is a habitual/continuous past construction 'kept going' or 'went on' (همی as a continuous/adverbial particle before the past verb رفت). هرگونه‌ای modifies داستان: 'every kind of story' or 'in every fashion the tale (went)'. Word order places modifier(s) before the noun and the verb at the end, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s608-g120: چه ... و چه ... is a correlative pair 'whether ... or ...'. از means 'from/of', بدنژاد 'low‑born/ignoble' and راستان 'the upright/righteous'. This clause lists the kinds of people/stories concerned: 'whether from the low‑born or from the righteous.'
segments.s608-g1: دگر هفته is an adverbial time phrase meaning 'another/a week later' placed before the accompaniment phrase. با + noun phrase (موبدان و ردان) marks accompaniment ('with the mōbeds and the beaters'); there is no verb here yet, so this whole segment functions as the temporal and circumstantial setting for the main verb in the next segment.
segments.s608-g2: به + نخچیر (direction/goal) indicates going to the hunting-ground; شد is the past-tense verbal marker from شدن ('became/Set out') used here to indicate that the king set off/went to hunt; شهریار جهان is the subject/title 'the king of the world'. Word order places the directional phrase before the verb and the title after it, a common classical Persian ordering.
segments.s608-g3: چنان بد که is a comparative/subordinating frame 'so/so bad that...' where که introduces the subordinate clause. ماهی here is used figuratively for a person (a beautiful/striking one) — in context the king — and به نخچیرگاه sets the location ('at the hunting-ground'). The phrase sets up the state described in the following clause.
segments.s608-g4: همی بود is the imperfect/past-state construction where همی gives a continuous/emphatic sense and بود is the past copula 'was.' میخواره is a predicate noun 'wine‑drinker/drunkard'; the clause ends in a coordinated phrase و با سپاه ('and with the army') where با marks accompaniment. Grammatically, بود + noun = 'he was X.'
segments.s608-g5: ز + نخچیر + کوه and likewise ز + نخچیر + دشت are prepositional noun phrases 'from the prey of the mountain' and 'from the prey of the plain'; the repeated ز highlights source ('from'). Word order is poetic: source (ز نخچیر کوه) before the verb phrase.
segments.s608-g6: گرفتن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'the taking/seizing'; ز اندازه اندر گذشت is an idiomatic clause meaning 'went beyond measure' — ز اندازه ('from measure', i.e. 'beyond measure'), اندر is a locative/poetic particle 'within/in', and گذشت (past of گذشتن) here means 'passed/exceeded.' Together: 'the taking exceeded all bounds.'
segments.s608-g7: سوی + شهر marks direction 'toward the city.' شد + شاددل here can be read as 'turned/became joyful' or 'was (made) joyful' — classical Persian uses شد with adjectives to indicate change-of-state or simple past: 'he became/was cheerful.' با سپاه is an accompanying prepositional phrase 'with his army.'
segments.s608-g8: This segment contains a sequence of short clauses: شب آمد ('night came' — آمد is past 'came'), به ره گشت ('he set out on the road' — به + ره = toward the way, and گشت here = 'went/set out'), and گیتی سیاه where گیتی (the world) + سیاه (black/dark) is a predicative phrase linked to the previous verb sense 'turned/became' (i.e. 'the world became dark'). The verbs are past and narrative.
segments.s608-g73: برو is the imperative/interjection 'go' placed at the line's start as an exclamation; پیرمرد is the noun 'old man' (subject or addressee). آفرین کرد is a light verb construction: آفرین (an exclamation 'bravo' / 'well done') + کرد (past tense of کردن) = 'praised' or 'exclaimed praise'. و گفت is the connective 'and said' linking this praise to the following reported speech.
segments.s608-g74: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'that ...'); این دختران = 'these maidens/young women' (demonstrative + plural noun). م here is the clitic of the first-person object (part of مرا) but is given separately in the word list; را is the Persian definite/object marker attached after the object (here marking emphasis). نیست is the negative copula 'is not' and جفت means 'match' or 'mate' — together: 'that these girls are not a match for me.'
segments.s608-g75: رسیده is a perfect/predicative adjective 'having reached / arrived (at)' used with an age expression. بدین سال literally 'to/at this year' (بدین = ب + دین here meaning 'to this'), so رسیده بدین سال = 'having reached this year/age'. دوشیزه‌اند is دوشیزه + اند (3rd-person plural copula) = 'they are maidens' (present-state).
segments.s608-g76: به here introduces the condition/state 'in/with respect to' (به دوشیزگی = 'in maidenhood'). نیز = 'also/likewise' adds emphasis. پاکیزه‌اند is پاکیزه + اند (they are clean/pure) — present copula in plural agreeing with دوشیزه‌اند.
segments.s608-g77: ولیکن is an archaic/poetic conjunction meaning 'but/yet'. ندارند is the present-negative 3rd-person plural of داشتن 'they do not have' (ندارند = they have not/they lack). چیزی فزون = 'anything additional / something more' (چیزی = 'something'; فزون = 'more' / 'additional'). Together: 'but they have no more of anything.'
segments.s608-g78: نگوییم is 1st-person-plural hortative/optative negative (let us not say / we should not speak). زین بیش چیزی کنون = 'from this (زین) more (بیش) anything (چیزی) now (کنون)' — idiomatically 'let us not say anything more of this now.' Note that زین is an archaic prepositional/partitive 'of/from this'.
segments.s608-g79: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (ب + او fused in older usage) introducing the indirect object of گفت. گفت (here a verb 'said') follows, then the speaker's name بهرام. کاین = که + این (that these/that which) introduces the object/topic, and هر چهار = 'all four' / 'each of the four' — together: 'To him Bahram said that these four ...'.
segments.s608-g80: به من ده is an imperative clause 'give to me' (به من = to me; ده = give — note that the word-list here omitted a separate 'ده' but the sense is imperative). وزین is an adjective 'weighty / substantial / dignified' modifying the following noun; بیش = 'more / besides'; دختر مکار = 'deceitful maidens' or 'cunning girls' (دختر = maiden/girl; مکار = deceitful). Altogether: 'Give (them) to me, and besides give (me) other weighty/cunning maidens.'
segments.s608-g49: This is an elliptical noun phrase meaning “one (was) named Moshk.” 'یکی' (one/a certain one) introduces an individual; 'مشک نام' is a name phrase where 'نام' (= “name”) follows the name and functions like English 'named'. The conjunction 'و' links to the next contrasted item and 'دگر' means 'the other/another'.
segments.s608-g50: Parallel structure to the previous segment: 'یکی نام نار' = 'one named Nar' (again with the noun + 'نام' pattern), 'و دگر سوسنک' = 'and another Susank.' The verb 'was/is' is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so these are nominal phrases giving the girls' names.
segments.s608-g51: 'بر شاه رفتند' is VSO ordering common in classical Persian: the verb 'رفتند' (3rd pl. past) + subject 'شاه' (the king) with preposition 'بر' meaning 'toward/to' here ('went to the king'). 'با دست‌بند' is an accompanying adverbial phrase 'with bracelets' (با + noun), placed after the verb.
segments.s608-g52: 'به رخ' ('in/to the face/countenance') introduces an adverbial/locative phrase describing appearance; 'چون بهار' is a simile 'like spring.' The second coordinate 'و به بالا بلند' uses 'به' + adverb 'بالا' + adjective 'بلند' to mean 'and (held) high/upward.' The clause lacks an explicit copula—typical poetic ellipsis—so these phrases modify how they appeared as they approached the king.
segments.s608-g53: 'یکی چامه گفتند' = 'one (of them) recited/sang an ode' — in Persian 'چامه گفتن' is a literary idiom meaning to recite or sing a poem. 'گفتند' is 3rd person plural past. 'بهرام را' with the object marker 'را' marks Bahram as the definite recipient/target of the recitation.
segments.s608-g54: This is an appositional/epithet phrase describing the addressee: 'شهنشاه' (the king) followed by attributes 'با دانش و نام' ('with learning and (with) renown'). The final 'را' ties this descriptive phrase back to the definite object marker in the previous segment (it all refers to Bahram).
segments.s608-g55: 'ز هر چار پرسید' literally 'from each of the four he asked' (the prepositional phrase 'ز هر چار' = 'from each of the four'), then the subject 'بهرام گور' follows; the verb 'پرسید' is 3rd singular past. Word order is flexible/classical (preposed object phrase, verb, then subject). 'گور' here is the epithet attached to Bahram (Bahram Gur).
segments.s608-g56: 'کزیشان' = 'which of them' (interrogative/relative 'کز' + plural clitic 'شان' referring back to the four). The embedded clause 'به دلش اندر افتاد شور' is idiomatic: 'a شور (passion/ardor) fell into his heart' — 'دلش' shows possession with the enclitic 'ش' (his), 'اندر افتاد' = 'fell into / befell.' Together this forms the question-object of 'پرسید.'
segments.s608-g57: که‌ای is a vocative/addressing particle here (akin to “O”); گلرخان is an epithet “rose‑faced” modifying the addressees; دختران is the plural noun “maidens/daughters” being addressed; که‌اید is an archaic present copula/identity question form (2nd/3rd‑person present) used here to ask “who are (you)?” — the clause is a direct vocative question.
segments.s608-g58: وزین here functions as an old/dialectal demonstrative/adverbial element pointing to ‘this/so (fire)’ (archaic usage attached to آتش); آتش افروختن is the infinitival/verbal noun construction ‘to kindle a fire’ (آتش object + infinitive افروختن); بر is a prepositional particle introducing the question of purpose/target and links to چه‌اید, and چه‌اید is the interrogative copular form asking ‘for what/why’ (literally ‘what are [you] for’).
segments.s608-g59: یکی at clause‑start is the indefinite ‘one’ / ‘someone’ (introducing a speaker); گفت is the reported‑speech verb ‘said’ (past) introducing the quotation; کای is a vocative particle ‘O’; سرو بالا is a descriptive compound — سرو ‘cypress’ used metaphorically for a tall/beautiful man and بالا ‘high/lofty’ intensifying it; سوار is the addressee ‘rider/horseman.’
segments.s608-g60: به ... ماننده is a comparative frame: به marks the standard/ground of comparison, هر چیز ‘in every respect’ (literally ‘to/for every thing’), ماننده ‘like, resembling’; شهریار is the noun of comparison ‘a king/sovereign.’ The whole phrase means ‘like a king in every way.’
segments.s608-g61: پدرمان is the possessed noun ‘our father’ (پدر + suffix مان); یکی is the indefinite determiner ‘a certain’ / ‘one’ introducing the profession; آسیابان is the noun ‘miller’; پیر is an adjective ‘old’ following the noun (typical Persian noun + adjective order).
segments.s608-g62: بدین (here represented in two pieces) is an archaic locative/demonstrative ‘in/at this’ (ب + این); کوه ‘mountain’ is the location; نخچیر ‘prey/booty’ is the object he obtains; گیرد is the present/habitual verb ‘takes/seizes’ (3rd person); به تیر is an instrumental phrase ‘with/by [the] arrow’ (به marking the instrument).
segments.s608-g63: بیاید is an archaic/present form meaning ‘he/it comes’ or ‘he arrives’ (here habitual/presentive); همانا is an emphatic adverb ‘verily/indeed’; چو is the subordinating temporal conjunction ‘when’; شب تیره شد is the temporal clause ‘night became dark’ with تیره (dark) + شد (past of ‘become’). The clause order places the temporal when‑clause after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s608-g64: ورا is an old pronominal/locative form ‘to him’ / ‘therefor’ introducing the affected person; دیده ‘eye’ here means ‘sight/his eye’; از is the preposition ‘from/out of’ indicating cause; تیرگی ‘gloom/darkness’ is the cause; خیره شد is a compound of adjective/participle خیره ‘dazed/staring’ plus شد ‘became’ — ‘his sight became dazzled/stunned.’
segments.s608-g65: This is an adverbial/time phrase. هم‌اندر (literally “together/at the same time”) + زمان (time) introduces the temporal setting: “at the same time.” آسیابان is the agent/owner “the miller.” ز کوه is a poetic prepositional phrase (ز = از) meaning “from the mountain,” modifying the coming/brought action in the next segment.
segments.s608-g66: بیاورد is a past narrative verb here (“brought”). نخچیر is the direct object meaning ‘prey/game’ or ‘spoil’; خود is a possessive/emphatic referring back to the miller (“his own”); با گروه is an accompanying-phrase “with a group/company.” Word order is fairly tight: verb (brought) + object (his prey) + adjunct (with company).
segments.s608-g67: چو ... دید is a temporal clause: چو = “when,” followed by the object phrase بهرام را (Bahrām marked by را as the object of دید). رخ را به خاک is the object + directional phrase of the next verb: رخ (face) with را (definite object marker) and به خاک indicating direction/location (“onto the ground/dust”).
segments.s608-g68: بمالید is the past tense verb “rubbed” (3rd person). The subject follows in inverted/poetic order: آن پیر آزاده پاک = “that old, noble, pure man.” Grammatically, the verb precedes its explicit subject here for emphasis; the action (rubbing the face on the earth) is an act of reverence.
segments.s608-g69: This is object–verb–subject word order typical of poetic prose: یکی جام زرین is the direct object “one/a golden cup.” بفرمود is the honorific/polite past verb meaning here “(the king) commanded/ordered (that ... be given).” شاه, the agent, follows the verb. یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral/“one” (= “a certain/one”).
segments.s608-g70: بدان پیر دادن is the complement of بفرمود: بدان = به + آن (“to that”), marking the recipient; پیر is ‘the elder’; دادن is the infinitive “to give,” so together “order(ed) to give to that elder.” The relative clause introduced by که (که آمد ز راه) modifies پیر: که introduces “who/that,” آمد (past “came”) + ز راه “from the road.” Note: the word-list split 'بدان' into 'ب' and 'دان' in the data; grammatically this is به + آن = 'to that', while 'دان' as listed does not match this usage (so its provided senses are not used here).
segments.s608-g71: بدو گفت = “he said to him” (بدو = to him; گفت = said). کاین is an archaic/poetic contraction (ک + این) meaning “that these” or “these”; چار خورشید روی is a descriptive noun phrase: چار = four, خورشید = sun, روی = faces — i.e. “the four sun‑faced ones” (a poetic way to describe very beautiful faces). Together this segment introduces quoted speech directed at the elder.
segments.s608-g72: چه داری is the main question “what do you have/what use do you have (for them)?” چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” هستند is the verb “they are,” and هنگام شوی is an idiomatic temporal phrase meaning “when the time comes/becomes” (شوی is the verb شدن ‘to become’ used in a temporal sense). Put together the temporal clause asks about the situation “when their time comes” (i.e. when they reach marriageable age), so the whole line asks rhetorically what the old man will do with those four young beauties at that time.
segments.s608-g129: کنون is a sentence‑initial adverb meaning “now.” دختران تو is the noun phrase “your daughters” (تو placed after the noun as the possessive). جفت is a predicate noun meaning “mates/paired (spouses).” وی‌اند is an archaic/copulative form meaning “they are” (functioning as the copula with the plural subject). The literal order is adverb — subject — predicate — copula.
segments.s608-g130: به آرام is an adverbial phrase “in peace/peacefully” (به used here to mark manner). اندر نهفت is a verbal/adjectival phrase meaning “hidden within” (اندر = in/within; نهفت = concealed). وی‌اند again supplies the copula “they are,” so the clause reads “they are peacefully hidden inside.”
segments.s608-g131: بدان is a prepositional/concessive particle here (bě + دان) used roughly as “by/with/considering that”; it introduces the list of attributes. روی = “face,” آن موی = “that hair,” و = “and,” آن راستی = “that uprightness/virtue.” The segment enumerates the qualities that justify the following statement.
segments.s608-g132: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here meaning “indeed” or “truly.” شاه را marks “the king” as a (grammatical) object/beneficiary (را is the definite/object marker). دختر آراستی is the verb phrase “you have adorned/provided a daughter” (آراستی = you adorned/prepared). Together the clause says that, by those qualities, you have in fact given/adorned a daughter for the king.
segments.s608-g133: شهنشاه بهرام is the nominal subject in apposition (“the emperor, Bahram”). داماد is the predicate noun “son‑in‑law.” تست here is an archaic/copular construction meaning ‘is (of) you’ — i.e. ‘he is your son‑in‑law’ (morphologically t(=2nd‑person marker) + est/copula in older Persian).
segments.s608-g134: به هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase “in/throughout every country/realm” (به + هر + کشوری). زین سپس is an idiomatic temporal phrase “from this/thereafter” (زین = ز این = from this; سپس = then/afterwards). یاد تست = “you are remembered” where یاد is “remembrance” used predicatively and تست supplies the copula (i.e. ‘you will be remembered’).
segments.s608-g135: ترا داد is the verb phrase “gave to you” (ترا = to/for you as object; داد = gave, past tense). این کشور و مرز پاک is the direct object “this country and the pure/clean border.” Word order: indirect object + verb + direct object is common in older Persian epic style.
segments.s608-g136: مخور غم is an imperative prohibition “do not grieve” (مخور literally “do not eat,” here idiomatically “do not consume yourself with grief”); که introduces the reason (“for/because”). رستی is perfect/resultive “you have been saved/delivered,” and ز اندوه و باک = “from sorrow and fear.” So the clause comforts the addressee: do not grieve, since you are freed from sorrow and fear.
segments.s608-g105: بر آواز is a prepositional phrase meaning “at/on the sound (voice) of”; the noun phrase این رامش (this Râmesh) modifies آواز and appears in normal determiner + noun order; دختران is the plural subject affected by that prepositional circumstance — the verb is omitted here and supplied by the following segment.
segments.s608-g106: ز مستی is another prepositional phrase “from/because of intoxication/ecstasy”; می‌آورد is the imperfective 3rd-singular of آوردن used here as a narrative/habitual (‘would bring/about’ or ‘caused’), with the logical subject (the song) understood from the previous phrase; و links the clause to رامشگران, indicating the comforters/peacemakers were likewise affected.
segments.s608-g107: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so”; گفت is the past-tense verb “said”; پس here means “then/thereupon”; آسیابان is the subject (the miller) and به زن is the indirect object 'to the woman' (به + noun). Note Persian allows verb before subject and places the indirect object after the subject.
segments.s608-g108: که‌ای زن is a vocative/address form (“O woman”); مرا is the object pronoun 'me' plus the object marker را split here (m + را), with مرا meaning “to me” or “for me”; داستانی is an indefinite/tale noun 'a story'; بزن is the imperative of زدن used idiomatically with داستان to mean 'tell (a story)' — literally 'strike/give a story', here 'tell me a tale'.
segments.s608-g109: که introduces reported content ('that...' or a clause); نیکیست is a nominal/predicative phrase meaning 'is good' (نیکی + است contracted), and فرجام is the subject 'the outcome/end' placed after the predicate for poetic word order; این‌گر بدی is a conditional/contrast 'but if this is bad' (این‌گر = 'if this is so / but if', بدی = 'evil/badness').
segments.s608-g110: زنش is زن + possessive suffix ش 'his wife'; گفت is the past-tense 'said'; کاری is the noun 'a deed/act' (here 'an act'), followed by بود 'was' (past copula) and ایزدی 'divine' describing the deed — together: 'his wife said, “it was a divine deed.”'
segments.s608-g111: نپرسید is the past negative 'did not ask' (ن- + پرسید); چون here functions as a temporal/concessive conjunction 'when/as'; دید is past 'saw'; مرد is the direct object 'the man'; از نژاد is a prepositional phrase 'of/from (his) lineage'—together: 'he did not ask when he saw the man was of a certain lineage.'
segments.s608-g112: نه ... is a contrastive negation 'not'; از خواسته 'from (any) desire/wish' indicates the cause that is denied; بر دلش بود یاد is a fixed poetic order meaning 'remembrance was upon his heart' (بر = on/upon, دلش = his heart, بود = was, یاد = memory/remembrance), i.e. he was reminded or recollected something, not because of desire.

sentences.s608-s9: On that side of the fire all the maidens had set up a single festive place upon the bank.
sentences.s608-s10: Each one wore on her head a diadem of flowers; they sat wherever they rested in peace.
sentences.s608-s11: They continually sang the martial lays of Khosrow, and from that place they struck up new tunes at every moment.
sentences.s608-s12: All were moon-faced and all ringleted; all (wore) robes of jewels and all (had) hair smelling of musk.

sentences.s608-s7: The emperor looked toward the brightness; to one side a joyful village came into view.
sentences.s608-s8: A certain mill was seen before the village; the great men sat scattered there.

sentences.s608-s60: Thus the tale went on in every manner, whether of the low‑born or of the righteous.
sentences.s608-s1: A week later the king of the world went hunting with the mōbeds and the beaters.

sentences.s608-s65: Now your daughters have been paired; they lie quietly concealed within.

sentences.s608-s68: He has given you this country and the clean frontier; do not grieve, for you have been delivered from sorrow and fear.
sentences.s608-s53: At the sound of this song the maidens were seized with ecstasy, and the comforters were as well.
